messages.content: Why does my github actions keep changing to the repo with the latest commit push, how can i set it to only deploy when main has recent pushes?

To ensure that your Github Action workflow only deploys when there are recent pushes to the main branch, you need to add a trigger condition in the workflow file. A trigger condition is used to specify when a particular workflow should be triggered.

You can add the following code at the beginning of your workflow file to specify that the workflow should only run on the main branch and when there are recent pushes:

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    # only run for recent pushes
    paths-ignore:
      - README.md # specify any file or directory to ignore
    # only run for pushes within the last 3 days
    if: github.event.pushed_at >= (github.event.head_commit.timestamp | subtract(259200))
```

This code specifies that the workflow only runs on the main branch, ignores pushes that modify the README.md file, and only runs for pushes within the last three days. You can adjust these parameters to suit your needs.

By adding this trigger condition, the workflow will only run when there are recent pushes to the main branch, and not for other branches with the latest commit push.
Teach me enough prisma so i am comfortable with it

Sure, I can teach you the basics of Prisma so that you can be comfortable using it in your projects. Prisma is an ORM and database toolkit that helps developers easily access and manipulate their databases in a type-safe way. 

Here is a step-by-step guide to getting started with Prisma:

1. Install the Prisma CLI: You can install Prisma CLI globally on your system to start using it. You can do this by running the following command in your terminal:
  ```
  npm install prisma -g
  ```
2. Initialize a new Prisma project: Once you have installed the Prisma CLI, you can create a new Prisma project by running the following command:
  ```
  prisma init
  ```
  This command will create a new Prisma project with some pre-defined boilerplate code. It will also create a `prisma` folder in your project that will contain the `schema.prisma` file.

3. Define your data model: The `schema.prisma` file is where you define your data model. You can define your data models using Prisma's declarative schema language. Here's an example schema that defines a `User` model:

  ```prisma
  datasource db {
    provider = "postgresql"
    url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
  }

  // Define your data model using Prisma's declarative schema language
  model User {
    id       Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
    email    String   @unique
    name     String?
    age      Int?
    posts    Post[]
    profile  Profile?
    createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  }

  model Post {
    id        Int     @id @default(autoincrement())
    title     String
    content   String?
    published Boolean @default(false)
    authorId  Int
    author    User    @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
    createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  }

  model Profile {
    id        Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
    bio       String
    userId    Int    @unique
    user      User   @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
    createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  }
  ```
  In this example, we have defined three data models: `User`, `Post`, and `Profile`. These models are related to each other using Prisma's `@relation` directive.

4. Generate database tables and Prisma client: Once you have defined your data model in the `schema.prisma` file, you can generate the tables in your database and Prisma client by running the following commands:
  ```
  prisma migrate dev
  ```
  This command generates a SQL migration script and applies it to your database to create the tables defined in the Prisma schema.

  ```
  prisma generate
  ```
  This command generates a Prisma client that you can use in your code to access the database. The Prisma client is generated based on the data model defined in your `schema.prisma` file.

5. Access and manipulate your data using Prisma: With the Prisma client generated, you can use it in your code to access and manipulate your data. Here's an example of how you can use the Prisma client to create a new user: 

  ```javascript
  const { PrismaClient } = require('@prisma/client')
  const prisma = new PrismaClient()

  async function createUser() {
    const newUser = await prisma.user.create({
      data: {
        name: 'John Doe',
        email: 'john@example.com',
        age: 25,
      },
    })
    console.log('New user created: ', newUser)
  }

  createUser()
  ```
  In this example, we are creating a new `User` record with name, email, and age fields. We are creating this record using the `create` method of the Prisma client.

And that's it! These are the basics of Prisma. Of course, there is so much more to learn and explore about Prisma, but this should give you a good starting point to get comfortable with it.
Now give me an exmaple of 3 or 4 related schemas and teach me about where, include , select and other most used schema properties

Sure! Let's create some example related schemas and explore some of the most commonly used Prisma schema properties:

Here's an example of 4 related data models: `User`, `Post`, `Comment`, and `Tag`.

```prisma
datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}
 
// User data model
model User {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  name      String
  email     String   @unique
  posts     Post[]
  comments  Comment[]
}
 
// Post data model
model Post {
  id          Int          @id @default(autoincrement())
  title       String
  content     String
  published   Boolean      @default(false)
  authorId    Int
  author      User         @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
  tags        Tag[]
  comments    Comment[]
  createdAt   DateTime     @default(now())
}
 
// Comment data model
model Comment {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  text      String
  authorId  Int
  author    User     @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
  postId    Int
  post      Post     @relation(fields: [postId], references: [id])
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
}
 
// Tag data model
model Tag {
  id       Int     @id @default(autoincrement())
  name     String  @unique
  posts    Post[]  @relation("TaggedPost")
}
```

The `User` and `Post` models have a one-to-many relationship because a user can have many posts, but a post can only have one author. Similarly, the `Post` and `Tag` models have a many-to-many relationship because a post can have many tags, and a tag can be associated with many posts. Finally, the `Comment` model has a many-to-one relationship with both the `User` and `Post` models because a comment belongs to one user and one post.

Now let's explore some of the commonly used Prisma schema properties:

- `@relation`: This property is used to define relationships between data models. In the above example, we have used the `@relation` property to define relationships between the `User`, `Post`, and `Comment` models.

- `@id`: This property is used to define the primary key field in a data model. In the above example, we have used the `@id` property to define the primary key fields in each data model.

- `@default`: This property allows you to specify a default value for a field. In the above example, we have used the `@default` property to specify default values for the `published` and `createdAt` fields.

- `@unique`: This property is used to specify that a field must have a unique value. In the above example, we have used the `@unique` property to specify that the `email` and `name` fields must have unique values.

- `@relation`: This property is used to describe the relationship between two data models. In the above example, we have used the `@relation` property to specify the relationship between `Post` and `Tag`. We named the relationship `TaggedPost`, which allows us to use it when querying data.

- `@include`: This property is used to include related data in a query. When you use `@include`, Prisma will return the related data as well. For example, if you include `comments` on a `posts` query, Prisma will return all the comments associated with each post.

- `@select`: This property is used to select only specific fields from a query. When you use `@select`, Prisma will only return the specified fields and ignore the others. For example, if you only select `id` and `title` on a `posts` query, Prisma will only return the `id` and `title` fields.

These are just a few of the commonly used Prisma schema properties, but there are many more to learn and explore as you work with Prisma.

How do i ssh into an ubuntu server from windows if have a public key , username and ip

To SSH into an Ubuntu server from Windows using a public key, username, and IP address, you will need an SSH client. Here are the steps to SSH into the server:

1. Download an SSH client like PuTTY from the official website https://www.putty.org/.

2. Once you have downloaded PuTTY, open it and you should see a screen with a field "Host Name" that you can enter the server's IP address.

3. Next, navigate to the "Connection" -> "SSH" tab on the left pane. Under this tab, you will see "Auth" option, click on it.

4. In the "Authentication Parameters" section of the "PuTTY Configuration" window, click the "Browse" button next to "Private key file for authentication" and select the private key file that corresponds to the public key that you have added to the server. Note that the key file must be in the PuTTY's `.ppk` format.

5. Navigate back to the "Session" tab and enter the username you have on the server in the "User Name" field.

6. Once you have provided the necessary information in the PuTTY configuration window, click the "Open" button to start the SSH connection.

7. You should receive a message warning you that this is the first time you are connecting to the server and that the server's authenticity cannot be verified. This is normal, click "Yes" to continue.

8. If everything is set up correctly, you should now be connected to the server via SSH.


Note: Make sure that the SSH port (usually port 22) on the server is open in the firewall and accessible from your Windows machine. Additionally, the public key must be added to the server's `authorized_keys` file to allow the SSH connection using the private key.

If you are using Ubuntu OS, you can directly use the terminal to SSH into an Ubuntu server using a public key, username, and IP address. Here are the steps to SSH into the server:

1. Open the terminal on your Ubuntu machine.

2. Use the `ssh` command followed by the username and IP address of the server, like this:
```
ssh username@ip_address
```

3. If this is the first time you are connecting to the server, you will be prompted to add the server's public key to your `known_hosts` file. Type "yes" to continue.

4. Next, you will be prompted for the passphrase for your private key (if you have set any). Enter the passphrase, if required.

5. Once you provide the passphrase, you should be logged into the remote server via SSH.

Note: Like I mentioned earlier, make sure that the SSH port (usually port 22) on the server is open in the firewall and accessible from your Ubuntu machine. If you face any issues with the SSH connection, try adding the `-v` option to the `ssh` command to troubleshoot the connection. The `-v` option will provide verbose output, which can help in diagnosing the issue.
How do add my public key to access from the terminal

To add your public key to an Ubuntu server to allow SSH access from the terminal, you need to follow these steps:

1. Generate an SSH key pair on your local machine if you haven't already done so. You can do this by running the following command in your terminal:

```
ssh-keygen
```

2. This will prompt you to enter a file name and passphrase for the key pair. You can simply press Enter to accept the defaults.

3. Once the key pair is generated, you need to copy the public key to the server by running the following command:

```
ssh-copy-id username@ip_address
```

Make sure to replace `username` and `ip_address` with the actual username and IP address of the server.

4. This command will copy your public key to the server and add it to the `authorized_keys` file on the server, which allows you to log in without being prompted for a password.

5. If you are prompted to enter your password during this process, enter your server password. Once you have entered the password, your public key will be copied to the server, and you should be able to log in without entering your password.

Now you can log in to the server via SSH by running the following command:

```
ssh username@ip_address
```

If everything is set up correctly, you should be able to log in to the server without using a password.
What if there is one public key for all users ?

If there is a single public key that is used for all users to access an Ubuntu server via SSH, you can add the public key to the `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` file on the server. Here's how you can do it:

1. Log in to the Ubuntu server as the root user or a user with sudo privileges.

2. Open the `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` file using your favorite text editor. For example, you can use the nano editor by running the following command:
```
sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config
```

3. Scroll down to the bottom of the file and add the following line to allow logins using the public key:
```
AuthorizedKeysFile  /etc/ssh/authorized_keys
```

4. Save and close the `sshd_config` file.

5. Create the `/etc/ssh/authorized_keys` file if it does not already exist by running the following command:
```
sudo touch /etc/ssh/authorized_keys
```

6. Append the public key to the `authorized_keys` file by running the following command:
```
sudo sh -c 'echo public_key_text >> /etc/ssh/authorized_keys'
```

Replace `public_key_text` with the actual public key.

7. Change the ownership and permissions of the `authorized_keys` file by running the following command:
```
sudo chown root:root /etc/ssh/authorized_keys
sudo chmod 600 /etc/ssh/authorized_keys
```

Now all users can log in to the server via SSH using the public key you added to the `/etc/ssh/authorized_keys` file.
How can users login for the above scenario

For the scenario where there is a single public key that is used for all users to access an Ubuntu server, every user must have the matching private key on their local machine. Here are the steps for users to log in to the server via SSH:

1. The users must create an SSH key pair on their local machine using the `ssh-keygen` command.

2. Once the key pair is generated, the user must copy their public key to the `/etc/ssh/authorized_keys` file on the server using the `ssh-copy-id` command. This step would need to be performed by a user with sudo access on the server, since they would need to have permission to modify the `/etc/ssh/authorized_keys` file.

3. Once the public key is added to the `authorized_keys` file, the user can log in to the server via SSH by running the following command on their local machine:

```
ssh user@server_ip_address
```

Replace `user` with the actual username of the user on the server, and `server_ip_address` with the IP address of the server.

Since the user's private key on their local machine matches the public key on the server's `authorized_keys` file, they should be able to log in without entering a password.

Note that every user who wants to access the server must have their public key added to the `authorized_keys` file on the server, and must have the matching private key on their local machine in order to authenticate successfully via SSH.
